questions.question: What does Sal learn about herself while watching this family interaction?

responses.content:
had her own agenda, just as I had had my own agenda that day my mother wanted me to walk with her. I couldn't see my own mother's sadness.

(23) "What?" Prudence said. "Come along and watch ?"

(24) "Yes, wouldn't that be nice?"

(25) "No!" Prudence said. "No, no, no. You can't. It would be awful."

(26) I heard the front door open and shut and Phoebe came in the kitchen waving a white envelope. "Guess what was on the steps?" she said.

(27) Mrs. Winterbottom took the envelope and turned it over and over before she slowly unsealed it and slipped out the message.

(28) "Oh," she said. "Who is doing this?" She held out the piece of paper: In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter?

(29) Prudence said, "Well, I have more important things to worry about, I can assure you. I know I'm going to blow those cheerleading tryouts, I just know it."

(30) On and on she went, until Phoebe said, "Cripes, Prudence, in the course of a lifetime, what does it matter?"

(31) At that moment, it was if a switch went off in Mrs. Winterbottom's brain. She put her hand to her mouth and stared out the window. She was invisible to Prudence and Phoebe, though. They did not notice.

(32) Phoebe said, "Are these cheerleading tryouts such a big deal? Will you even remember them in five years?"

(33) "Yes!" Prudence said. "Yes, I most certainly will."

(34) "How about ten years? Will you remember them in ten?"

(35) "Yes!" Prudence said.

(36) As I walked home, I thought about the message. In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter? I said it over and over. I wondered about the mysterious messenger, and I wondered about all the things in the course of a lifetime that would not matter. I did not think cheerleading tryouts would matter, but I was not so sure about yelling at your mother. I was certain, however, that if your mother left, it would be something that mattered in the whole long course of your lifetime.

questions.question: What is the central idea of “Everybody Jump (from What If?)?”

responses.content: A. When people work together, they can accomplish amazing physical feats like moving a planet.

questions.question: Highlight ONE sentence or phrase that shows an example of when Margaret Bourke-White's hardworking attitude helped advance her career.

responses.content: she fel proud of her work
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'she felt proud of her work' directly expresses a positive feeling (pride) toward Margaret's work as a photojournalist, which matches the text's depiction of her as passionate, dedicated, and a trailblazer. The answer is concise but meets the threshold for a correct response, as it clearly states Margaret's positive orientation toward her work.

questions.question: Based on paragraphs 10-12, what can you infer was important to Margaret as a photojournalist?

responses.content: At first, several people at the company wondered if a woman could stand up to the intense heat and generally dirty and gritty conditions inside a steel mill.
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is: 'At first, several people at the company wondered if a woman could stand up to the intense heat and generally dirty and gritty conditions inside a steel mill.' This is an exact match to one of the pre-approved correct answers. The highlight is within the character limit, directly answers the question by showing an example of when Margaret's hardworking attitude was tested and helped her advance her career, and is from the correct section of the text. Therefore, it is correct.

questions.question: Based on paragraph 14, how did Margaret influence future generations of women?

responses.content: Margaret cared about taking pictures of the war, steel mill, and social issues
responses.bucket_rationale: The response identifies that Margaret cared about taking pictures of the war, steel mill, and social issues. This matches the key elements from paragraphs 10-12, which describe her as a war correspondent, documenting complex events, and photographing social issues. The answer is specific, text-based, and accurate, meeting the threshold for 'correct.'
